title: Queue Chalk
desc: When the waiting court stretches, someone has to redraw where the line actually is.

steps.text:
The queue court outside the Turning Rooms is longer than it was an hour ago and shorter than it will be by lunch. Tiles slide, steps double back, and alcoves that once held two benches decide they are broad enough for a whole family. Without constant attention, the waiting line would become a social experiment with injuries.

A floor guide hands you a chalk satchel and points to the moving stone. "The chalk only takes on surfaces that currently believe they are floors," she says. "That sounds like a joke, but it will save you time." Around you, visitors drift in loose uncertainty, trying to determine where patience is meant to stand.

Today's task is simple in the way carnival tasks are always simple: redraw the line, restore the courtesy circles, and keep strangers from wandering into a service fold that currently leads to a room full of accordion music and no exits.

options.label: Redraw the route through the folded arcade
Reset the bell posts that mark safe waiting spots

steps.text:
The chalk goes down bright white against the dark tiles, then warms to gold as the floor settles under it. You lay arrows through a corridor that curls around itself like ribbon, mark standing circles at the corners, and redraw the patient zigzag every time the outer wall decides it would rather be closer.

It is strangely satisfying work. A line is a promise, and in the carnival promises matter even more than usual. Visitors stop hesitating once the path is visible. Two UnderWorlders who were prepared to argue about whose turn came next fall silent the moment you complete the next set of circles. The floor guide taps one of your fresh marks with her boot. "Good. That one should survive the noon stretch."

By the time the court expands again, the crowd is moving in clean, slow order. The line is still impossible, but it is impossible on purpose. That turns out to be enough.

options.label: Step aside and watch the line hold

steps.text:
The bell posts are little brass stands weighted to stay upright even when the local down tilts. Each one carries a cup that rings softly when its patch of floor is safe to wait on and falls silent when the court is about to shift. Your job is to move them with the geometry and keep the notes in the right order.

You carry each post to the new corners, test the cup with a fingernail, and adjust the hanging weight until the tone steadies. When the court stretches, the bells seem to slide across the air before they settle into place again. Children in line listen for the sound and shuffle forward without needing to be told. Adults pretend they were doing that anyway.

It takes most of the morning, but by the end the entire queue moves to music so soft most visitors do not notice it consciously. They just stop stepping where they should not. In a place built on confusion, that feels almost luxurious.

options.label: Leave the bells humming in order
